physicalDetails.detail: Not seen on page; the chapter presents him through reputation as the powerful archmage whose name immediately changes the weight of Jaina’s detention.

Chapter 22 finally puts Antonidas bodily on the page again: travel-worn instead of tower-composed, under a cloak rather than formal robes, with service-passage torchlight and palace stone making him look thinner, older, and more frightened than distance ever allowed.
Chapter 23 reduces Antonidas to a vanishing margin-signature: the blue-white light withdrawing behind the service latch after he has opened the last door he can open for them.

Chapter 24 makes him visibly present but politically chained: old, travel-worn, and furious behind restraint, forced to watch the tribunal while every intervention he can make is cut down to objection, warning, and witness.

voiceMarkers.detail: Chapter 8 finally gives Antonidas a live on-page voice, and the key marker is subtraction: the careful academic padding falls away once Jaina speaks Kel'Thuzad's name.
Chapter 9 reduces Antonidas to institutional afterimage: what reaches the page is copyist-flat methodology language rather than the stripped urgency of his chapter 8 stone response.

Chapter 14 gives Antonidas a tired mentor-voice under institutional load: the report questions stay exact, the plea “Come home” lands as fear before rank, and the dry impatience returns only after he is sure Jaina will keep breathing.

Chapter 18 keeps the same mentor register but thins it further: fragmentary reports, immediate extraction offers, and the repeated attempt to build a road home out of language that is no longer large enough for Stratholme.
Chapter 19 turns the voice into one last fragile tether: concern, authority, and extraction logic all still present, but no longer enough to keep Jaina on the line.

Chapter 20 leaves Antonidas voiceless but present as remembered instruction: the mentor standard Jaina keeps testing herself against once the sea crossing proves endurance can become its own form of error.

His voice in chapter22 alternates between stripped mentor urgency and forced operational plainness: only Jaina at first, then tell me the plan in words a judge could follow, then the quieter admission that he knows exactly what failure looks like.

beliefs.belief: If Kel'Thuzad is attached to the plague, then delay is already more dangerous than political caution.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by the way Antonidas responds on the stone in chapter 8, stripping counsel down to survival, record-keeping, and immediate action.

beliefs.belief: The Council wants the proof laid out cleanly before wider commitment, even after Kel'Thuzad's name has entered the field.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active in chapter 9 as off-page institutional pressure rather than visible conviction.

beliefs.belief: If Jaina has only partial proof inside a hostile institution, survival and written record matter more than one more elegant answer.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 10 by the content of the sending-stone exchange.

beliefs.belief: Practical necromancy crosses an ethical line because once the theory touches real bodies, scholarship collapses into violation.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 12 through Kel'Thuzad's own hostile summary of Antonidas and the Council's position.

beliefs.belief: Even partial theory must be acted on immediately once the larger chain above the plague is known.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by chapter 14. Antonidas asks for the fuller version, hears the limits, and still promises to move what he can instead of waiting for perfect formalization.

beliefs.belief: Jaina can still be called back into Dalaran's protection if he can reach her in time.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active in chapter 19, but the chapter makes clear his reach no longer governs her choice.

beliefs.belief: A mage's discipline includes knowing when to stop before effort becomes self-destruction.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter20 through Jaina's remembered Antonidas-trained standards even without a live exchange.

beliefs.belief: By the time Arthas is back in Capital City, formal authority and public warning are largely dead lanes; only tiny covert action at the palace margin may still matter.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter22 by his road-tired presence in the city, Uther’s blocked audiences, and the fact that his help can extend only to one softened service lock.

goalsHistory.underlying: Functions in the chapter as Jaina’s credential and political danger marker rather than as an active participant.
Still functions primarily as institutional gravity over Jaina’s choices, now complicated by the fact that she acted without his live authorization.

By chapter 7 he reads as the distant archmage whose judgment was strong enough to tell Corin help would come if the pattern held, extending his authority into the city without putting him physically there.

goalsHistory.active: Start whatever Kirin Tor response he can from Dalaran now that Kel'Thuzad is named and Andorhal has been confirmed as active deployment machinery.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep Jaina alive long enough to carry more truth out of the field than the institution can currently reach on its own.

goalsHistory.active: Off-page and indirect; chapter 9 presents him through Dalaran's methodology review rather than through direct intervention.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep the Kirin Tor response inside process even while the field has already outrun process.

goalsHistory.active: Hold the line to Jaina long enough to get her evidence out alive, even if that means settling for partial proof and delayed action.
goalsHistory.underlying: Prevent Kel'Thuzad's intellectual legacy from claiming Jaina the way it once claimed his former colleague.

goalsHistory.active: Keep the line to Jaina open, move whatever help he can from fragmentary Stratholme reports, and wait for the next scrap of truth she can still send.
goalsHistory.underlying: Bring her home if possible without losing the larger answer the field is forcing her to chase.
Persists in chapter20 as the internalized mentor discipline Jaina still uses to measure safe magical practice even after direct contact has broken.

goalsHistory.active: Hold the east-side service arch open for as short a window as possible and keep a last covert lane into the palace alive.
goalsHistory.underlying: Get Jaina and her answer close enough to Arthas to matter before court ritual and blade-presence close every remaining opening.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Keep public pressure on the tribunal's result from the outside and turn any corroborating witness into leverage before Frostmourne's timeline outruns procedure entirely.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Save Jaina and contain the blade despite arriving too late to prevent the verdict.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Off-page and unknown; chapter 2 makes clear only that he does not know Jaina’s position in real time.
Off-page and unknown; chapter 7 only proves that his long-range read of the danger was better than the bureaucracy below him.
Alarmed, guilty, and stripped down to urgency; chapter 8 makes him sound like a man hearing an old failure return in a far worse form.
Unreadable at distance. The chapter leaves only institutional lag, not personal voice, on the page.

Anxious, guilty, and reduced to fragile contact. Chapter 10 makes him feel like a man trying to hold his apprentice through static while knowing the room around her belongs to an older failure of his own generation.
Frightened, affectionate, and painfully aware that concern, office, and distance are all failing in different ways at once.
Off-page and unreadable. In chapter20 he survives primarily as remembered care and training pressure inside Jaina rather than as a live emotional participant.
Frightened, road-worn, and functioning past the point where authority offers comfort, with fear only briefly visible before he forces it back under work.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Furious, constrained, and conscious of failure on two fronts at once: mentor-failure toward Jaina and institutional failure toward the blade itself.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Recognizes Kel'Thuzad's name instantly from Jaina's fragments instead of demanding a full lecture before reacting.
Orders Jaina to leave Andorhal alive and to put everything in writing, showing that his first response is now evidence survival and body survival, not procedure.
Tells Jaina to leave the Barov line with material instead of waiting for complete certainty, proving he now understands the field is outrunning any clean institutional timetable.
Chapter 12 adds no live decision from Antonidas, but it confirms the earlier ban he helped enforce was a conscious moral refusal, not ignorance of the theorem.
Demands the fuller report immediately once the second call opens.
Presses Jaina on whether the plague can be stopped and accepts an incomplete answer instead of pretending it is useless.
Tells her to come home, then concedes the refusal and shifts into what action he can still take from Dalaran.
Offers immediate riders and a cleared route home the moment he confirms she is alive after Stratholme.
Accepts 'not yet' once more and shifts to the narrower practical instructions he can still give: keep the stone on you, write scraps while you can.
Takes Jaina's second call and tries once more to bring her back inside Dalaran's protection before she ends the exchange.
Makes no live decision on page in chapter20, but his earlier teaching remains active enough to shape how Jaina judges the point where magical strain stops being disciplined and becomes reckless.
Reaches Capital City in person with two Kirin Tor magi, calls Jaina over the stone from inside the city, and gives her the truth that all the warning lanes arrived too late.
Arranges the palace-margin meeting, asks for the anti-blade plan in plain tactical language, and commits his magi to softening one service lock even though he cannot go farther in himself.
Objects to the tribunal's framing, tries to put the blade's ongoing danger on the public record, and keeps showing up even after the court reduces him to a compromised witness.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Not demonstrated on page; his authority is implied by the way his name instantly escalates the stakes.
Can hear a partial field brief over a sending stone and identify the full threat load of a name faster than anyone else in the room.
Maintains a usable sending-stone conversation through heavy ward interference long enough to give Jaina operationally relevant guidance.
Chapter 12 retroactively sharpens one Antonidas strength that matters to future scenes: he understood necromantic theory well enough to know exactly where practical use became intolerable.
Extracts strategic clarity from a stressed long-distance report without losing the human bond inside the exchange.
Can pivot from mentor plea to institutional movement in a single conversation when the field leaves him no cleaner option.
Can make a sending-stone exchange feel simultaneously like an extraction corridor, a strategic debrief, and a conversation between teacher and apprentice.
Can still project steadiness over distance even when the person on the other end is already choosing a road beyond his authority.
His training remains durable enough to govern Jaina's self-assessment even in his physical absence and after the line to Dalaran is gone.
Can still build a covert operational lane inside hostile political space while stripped of formal authority.
Keeps strategic clarity under extreme emotional pressure, reducing palace access, magical sensing, and legal risk to the narrow facts that matter.
Can hold a line of institutional dissent inside hostile public proceedings, staying precise about magical danger even when the room is determined to translate warning into self-interest.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Cyndia
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Used as a credential marker: apprentice to Antonidas.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Invoked as the mentor she left a note for, not as an authority who actually sent her.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Corin
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Used as distant validating authority: Antonidas said if the pattern held Jaina would come, which turns the archmage into proof that the warning had higher-level recognition even if local systems stalled.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Chapter 8 uses him less as an off-page credential than as the one person who can understand immediately what Kel'Thuzad's return means.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Kel'Thuzad
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Speaks Antonidas's name as shared-teacher history and institutional wound, with brief warmth that makes the critique more intimate rather than less.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Chapter 14 opens with “Archmage” as reporting protocol, then shifts to “Antonidas” only once the line has become personal and irreversible.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: By chapter 19 he remains intimate mentor-presence rather than remote office, which makes the severed call land harder.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses Antonidas almost immediately because the situation has burned away any room for ceremonial distance.

howSheAddressesOthers.pattern: Opens with her name alone, the purest version of the bond he has left.
Addresses her as Lady Windrunner when the plan’s bodily cost forces him into open acknowledgment of what he is asking.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Keeps turning from torchlit passage to service door to Jaina’s face, putting fear away by focusing on logistics, locks, and the people still in front of him.

knowledge.detail: The chapter establishes that Jaina is his apprentice, making her detention more explosive than a nameless border trespass.
Chapter 2 establishes that Jaina left him a note before departing and that he does not know her whereabouts in real time, which strips his name of any protective cover story for her border crossing.

Chapter 7 implies Antonidas had already taken the pattern seriously enough to prepare Corin for Jaina’s arrival if the city evidence kept holding, which makes him feel more accurate than the institutions beneath him.
Now knows the plague architect is Kel'Thuzad, operating somewhere north of Andorhal.
Knows the field team is already trailing deployment tempo and that any Kirin Tor action he starts will begin behind the immediate crisis.
By chapter 9, Antonidas's side of the line reaches Jaina as review language and methodology pressure instead of immediate field-matching urgency.
Now knows the Barov estate and Caer Darrow hold live research infrastructure built on recognizably Kirin Tor-derived notation and method.
Knows Jaina still lacks the final waking step and that the answer lies somewhere lower inside Scholomance than the material she can safely carry off at first pass.
Chapter 12 confirms that Kel'Thuzad still thinks in Antonidas-shaped notation and still frames the old ban as a choice against practical violation rather than a failure to understand the theory.
The chapter also confirms how much of Jaina's own training still runs through Antonidas's habits: she reads Kel'Thuzad's room as her teacher's discipline turned inside out.
Now knows the Lich King chain, the Burning Legion frame, the body-learning mechanism of the plague, and the fact that Jaina has only a narrow intervention model rather than a city-scale answer.
Knows Jaina refused recall and is riding toward Arthas and Stratholme instead of back toward Dalaran.
Now has fragmentary word of Stratholme, knows the grain alone cannot explain the city-scale answer, and understands that Jaina is still riding south only because the real model is now pulling north.
Now knows Jaina is turning north, that ordinary recall language has failed, and that his authority no longer reaches far enough to reroute her.
By chapter20 his practical knowledge on page is indirect: Jaina still carries his standards for magical overreach and uses them while confronting the sea-crossing resonance.

Now knows Arthas entered Capital City days earlier, public audiences have resumed under celebration, Northrend guards are already rotating inside the palace, and Uther’s formal attempts to intervene have failed.
Learns Jaina’s anti-blade framework in full and understands that the question is no longer theory but who can buy the spell its seconds.
By the time chapter23 opens, Antonidas has already been forced out of the line of action and can contribute only the fading proof that he got them this far.
Now knows the tribunal will convict despite the magical danger, that Frostmourne remains active in custody, and that his own authority inside Lordaeron is no longer enough to force a clean response.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Still off-stage, but now differentiated from the institution around him: important enough to matter, absent enough not to have prevented Jaina’s unauthorized ride.
Still off-stage in chapter 7, but now he reads less like a dead credential and more like remote strategic authority whose judgment outran local procedure.

Chapter 8 finally makes Antonidas an active voice instead of pure institutional shadow. He remains the mentor who believes Jaina, but now belief carries the extra wound of hearing Kel'Thuzad's name and knowing his own institution helped shape the mind behind the plague.
Chapter 9 pushes Antonidas back behind the institution. After chapter 8's personal urgency, he is now felt as lagging review language arriving too slowly for the people still bleeding in the field.
Chapter 12 keeps Antonidas off-stage but restores his moral relevance. He is the absent teacher present in notation, in prohibition, and in the exact line Kel'Thuzad defines himself against.

Chapter 14 pulls Antonidas back out of abstraction and into frightened mentorhood. He remains institutionally too far away and too slow for the road, but the line is emotionally immediate again: he hears the danger, asks for more, and cannot make her come home.

Chapter 18 keeps Antonidas in the same tragic role but sharpens it: he is still emotionally immediate and strategically real, yet now even home sounds too small for what Jaina is carrying. He can offer routes, riders, and warmth. He cannot solve the north question for her.

Chapter 20 moves Antonidas one stage farther from live intervention and one stage deeper into internalized influence. He cannot reach Jaina directly, but his training still shapes the limits she is supposed to recognize.

Chapter 22 pulls Antonidas back from internalized mentorship into one last active field role: not rescuer, not institution, but the exhausted elder who can still open a side door and make the younger pair walk through it.

arc.currentPosition: Chapter 24 returns Antonidas to the room only to prove how little authority still answers to truth. He is no longer off-stage or merely mentoring; he is the elder expert forced to watch politics convict the right witnesses while the real emergency keeps worsening nearby.
